docs.title: Edu Platform
docs.text: Learn practical automation and system thinking using open-source tools Stat with Python for Operations.

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text:
This is the first server less webdevelopment, I have created using only open source tool.

The mission here is to create educational site with open source technologies that helps learn python science or pretty much anything that I teach.

docs.title: Energy in a Theme Park
docs.text: There are a lot of things happening in a theme park. Almost everything that happens involves energy in some way. For example, your body needs energy to stay alive, and to allow you to move around. Energy is needed to make all the rides in the theme park work. The energy needed is stored in food and in fuels, such as petrol.

docs.title: 1. Which of the rides shown in photo A needs the most energy and which the least energy? Explain your answers.

docs.text:
a) Write down five different things that are happening in photos A and B that need energy.

b) How is energy provided for these things to happen?

a) Write down five things you did yesterday that needed energy.

b) Which of these things do you think needed the most energy, and which needed the least energy? How do you know?

Note: Since photos A and B are not provided, answers to questions referencing them should be based on typical theme park observations (e.g., roller coasters, carousels, food stalls, lighting, sound systems, etc.).

docs.title: How Do Our Bodies Use Energy?
Introduction
docs.text:
Humans and other animals need energy to live. We use energy for:

  • Growth – building new cells and tissues
  • Repair – healing wounds, replacing worn-out cells
  • Movement – walking, running, breathing, even blinking
  • Keeping warm – maintaining body temperature (especially in cold environments)

Our bodies get this energy from food.

docs.title: Units of Energy
docs.text:
Unit Symbol Equivalent Joule J Base unit of energy Kilojoule kJ 1 kJ = 1000 J

Example: The energy needed to lift an apple from the floor onto a table is about 1 J. Most foods contain much more — so we use kilojoules (kJ).

docs.title: Reading Nutrition Labels
docs.text:
Nutrition labels show how much energy (and nutrients) are stored in food. They usually give values:

  • Per serving (e.g., per bun, per burger)
  • Per 100g (for easy comparison between products)

docs.title: Bun (65g)
docs.text: Nutrient Per bun (65g) Per 100g Energy 544 kJ / 130 kcal 837 kJ / 200 kcal Protein 6 g 9.2 g Carbohydrate 21 g 32.3 g Fat 2.5 g 3.85 g Fibre 1.2 g 1.8 g Sodium 0.2 g 0.3 g

docs.title: Falafel Burger (125g)
docs.text:
Nutrient Per burger (125g) Per 100g Energy 1990 kJ / 476 kcal 1592 kJ / 381 kcal Protein 17 g 13.6 g Carbohydrate 63 g 50 g Fat 15 g 12 g Fibre 7 g 5.6 g Sodium 2 g 1.6 g

Note: “of which sugar” or “of which saturates” refers to subcategories within carbs/fats.

docs.title: Example 1: Energy in 100g of falafel burger
docs.text:
From the label:

Per 100g → Energy = 1592 kJ

So, 100g of falafel burger contains 1592 kJ of energy.

docs.title: Example 2: If Mark eats two burgers meals (each meal = 1 falafel burger + 1 bun), How much total energy mark use up?
docs.text:
Step 1: Find energy per full meal (burger + bun)

  • Falafel burger (125g): 1990 kJ
  • Bun (65g): 544 kJ

→ Total per meal = 1990 + 544 = 2534 kJ

Step 2: Multiply by 2 meals

→ 2534 × 2 = 5068 kJ

Mark consumes 5068 kJ of energy from eating two falafel burgers with buns.

docs.title: Why Do Different People Need Different Amounts of Energy?
docs.text:
Your body’s energy needs depend on:

  • Age – children and teens need more for growth
  • Activity level – exercise burns more energy than watching TV
  • Body size – larger bodies need more energy to maintain
  • Gender – males often need slightly more due to muscle mass
  • Health status – illness or recovery may increase needs

If you do a lot of exercise, you need more energy than if you spend most of your time watching television.

docs.title: Question 1: Why does your body need food?
docs.text: Answer: Food provides energy for living processes: growing, repairing tissues, moving, and staying warm. It also supplies nutrients like protein, vitamins, and minerals essential for health.

docs.title: Question 2a: How much energy does 100g of falafel burger contain? Give your answer in kilojoules.
docs.text: Answer: From the nutrition label: → 1592 kJ

docs.title: Question 2b: Mark eats two burgers (each burger is one falafel burger in a bun). How much energy is in the food he eats?
docs.text:
Answer: Energy per burger + bun = 1990 kJ + 544 kJ = 2534 kJ For two meals: 2534 × 2 = 5068 kJ

Final Answer: 5068 kJ

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text: Energy is measured in joules (J) and kilojoules (kJ); 1 kJ = 1000 J Food labels show energy content per serving and per 100g Your body uses energy for growth, repair, movement, and warmth Active people need more energy than sedentary people *Always check both components when calculating total energy (e.g., burger + bun!)

docs.title: Study Tip
docs.text:
Practice reading real food labels at home! Compare snacks, drinks, or meals — calculate total energy for different portions. This helps build real-world math and science skills.

Page Reference: [Insert page number if known] Topic: Human Biology / Energy in Living Systems

docs.title: Nutrition Information Table
docs.text: Food Item Typical Values Per Serving Per 100g Cheddar Cheese Energy 336 kJ (per 30g slice) 1120 kJ Protein 7.5 g 25 g Fat 10 g 33.3 g Banana Energy 371 kJ (per 100g) 371 kJ Carbohydrate 23 g 23 g Sugar 12 g 12 g Peanut Butter Energy 620 kJ (per tablespoon/20g) 3100 kJ Protein 5 g 25 g Fat 10 g 50 g Apple Energy 218 kJ (per 100g) 218 kJ Carbohydrate 14 g 14 g Fibre 2.4 g 2.4 g Chocolate Bar Energy 1050 kJ (per 50g bar) 2100 kJ Sugar 25 g 50 g Fat 15 g 30 g Orange Juice Energy 188 kJ (per 100ml) 188 kJ Sugar 8 g 8 g Vitamin C 50 mg 50 mg Bread Slice Energy 314 kJ (per 40g slice) 785 kJ Carbohydrate 20 g 50 g Protein 3.6 g 9 g

docs.title: Level 1: Basic Ratio Calculations
docs.text:
Q1. Cheddar cheese contains 1120 kJ per 100g. Calculate the energy in: - a) 50g of cheddar cheese - b) 200g of cheddar cheese - c) 75g of cheddar cheese

Q2. Banana contains 371 kJ per 100g. If Sarah eats a banana weighing 150g, how much energy does she consume?

Q3. Peanut butter contains 3100 kJ per 100g. Calculate the energy in: - a) 50g of peanut butter - b) 25g of peanut butter - c) 10g of peanut butter

docs.title: Level 2: Intermediate Calculations
docs.text:
Q4. A chocolate bar contains 2100 kJ per 100g. - a) How much energy is in a 30g chocolate bar? - b) How much energy is in a 75g chocolate bar? - c) Tom eats 1.5 chocolate bars (each bar is 50g). Calculate total energy consumed.

Q5. Orange juice contains 188 kJ per 100ml. Calculate the energy in: - a) 200ml of orange juice - b) 150ml of orange juice - c) 350ml of orange juice - d) A small carton containing 250ml

Q6. Bread contains 785 kJ per 100g. Each slice weighs 40g. - a) Calculate the energy in one slice of bread - b) Calculate the energy in 3 slices of bread - c) Calculate the energy in a whole loaf containing 20 slices

docs.title: Level 3: Advanced Multi-Step Problems
docs.text:
Q7. Emma makes a sandwich with: - 2 slices of bread (40g each) - 30g of cheddar cheese - 15g of peanut butter

Calculate the total energy content of her sandwich.

Q8. Jake's breakfast includes: - 1 banana (120g) - 1 slice of bread (40g) - 200ml of orange juice

Calculate the total energy Jake consumes for breakfast.

Q9. Compare the energy content: - a) Which has more energy: 50g of chocolate or 100g of banana? - b) How much more energy does it contain?

Q10. A snack box contains: - 1 apple (150g) - 20g of cheddar cheese - 10g of peanut butter

  • a) Calculate the total energy in the snack box
  • b) If you wanted to reduce the energy by 200 kJ, which item would you remove or reduce?

docs.title: Level 4: Real-World Problems
docs.text:
Q11. Daily Energy Requirements:

Person Daily Energy Need Child (8-10 years) 8000 kJ Teenager (13-15 years) 10000 kJ Adult (moderate activity) 10500 kJ

Lisa (a teenager) eats: - Breakfast: 2 slices of bread with 20g peanut butter + 200ml orange juice - Lunch: Sandwich (2 bread slices + 40g cheese) + 1 apple (120g) - Snack: 30g chocolate bar - Dinner: To be calculated

  • a) Calculate Lisa's energy intake from breakfast, lunch, and snack
  • b) How much energy can she still consume at dinner to meet her daily requirement?

Q12. Weight Management:

Tom wants to gain weight and needs an extra 2000 kJ per day. Which option would provide closer to 2000 kJ? - Option A: 100g cheddar cheese + 1 banana - Option B: 50g peanut butter + 1 apple - Option C: 100g chocolate + 200ml orange juice

Show your calculations for each option.

docs.title: Level 3 Answers
docs.text:
A7. - 2 bread slices = 314 × 2 = 628 kJ - 30g cheese = 1120 × 0.3 = 336 kJ - 15g peanut butter = 3100 × 0.15 = 465 kJ - Total = 628 + 336 + 465 = 1429 kJ

A8. - Banana (120g) = 371 × 1.2 = 445.2 kJ - Bread (40g) = 314 kJ - Orange juice (200ml) = 188 × 2 = 376 kJ - Total = 445.2 + 314 + 376 = 1135.2 kJ

A9. - a) 50g chocolate = 2100 × 0.5 = 1050 kJ - 100g banana = 371 kJ - Chocolate has more energy - b) Difference = 1050 - 371 = 679 kJ more

A10. - a) Apple (150g) = 218 × 1.5 = 327 kJ - Cheese (20g) = 1120 × 0.2 = 224 kJ - Peanut butter (10g) = 3100 × 0.1 = 310 kJ - Total = 327 + 224 + 310 = 861 kJ - b) Remove 10g peanut butter (310 kJ) or reduce cheese

docs.title: Formula Reminder
docs.text: To calculate energy for any mass:

docs.title: Energy Balance and Weight
docs.text:
A balanced diet provides the correct amount of energy and nutrients for health. The relationship between energy intake and body weight is governed by the principle of energy balance:

  • Energy Surplus: If Energy In (Food) > Energy Out (Activity + Basic Metabolism), the body stores the excess energy as fat, leading to weight gain.
  • Energy Deficit: If Energy In < Energy Out, the body uses stored fat for energy, leading to weight loss and becoming thinner.

docs.title: Factors Affecting Energy Requirements
docs.text:
Not everyone needs the same amount of energy. Requirements vary based on:

  • Age: Children and teenagers need energy for growth and development.
  • Activity Level: Physical activity burns energy. Sedentary people need less than active people.
  • Physiological State: Pregnant women need extra energy for the growth of the fetus.

docs.title: Data Analysis: Graph C
docs.text:
The bar chart shows how average energy requirements change with age and activity.

  • Trend: Energy needs generally increase from age 5 to adulthood.
  • Activity Impact: "Very active adults" require significantly more energy (~15,000 kJ) compared to standard adults (~10,000 kJ).

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
a) Suggest why a teenager needs more energy than a 5-year-old child.

  • Growth: Teenagers undergo rapid growth spurts (puberty), requiring significant energy to build new tissue and bone.
  • Body Size: A teenager has a larger body mass than a 5-year-old, requiring more energy for basic maintenance (metabolism).
  • Activity: Teenagers are often more physically active than young children.

b) Why do you think a pregnant woman needs more energy from food than a woman who is not pregnant?

  • Energy is required for the growth and development of the fetus (baby).
  • Energy is needed for the growth of the placenta and the enlargement of the uterus and breasts.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
a) Write down these people in order of who needs the least energy to the most energy: baby, fire-fighter, secretary, 11-year-old child.

Order: Baby < 11-year-old child < Secretary < Fire-fighter

b) Explain your answer to part a.

  • Baby: Has the lowest total energy requirement because they have a very small body mass and spend most of their time sleeping (low activity).
  • 11-year-old child: Needs more than a baby due to growth and higher activity levels (playing/school), but less than an adult due to smaller body size (See Graph C: Age 11 is lower than Adult).
  • Secretary: An adult has a larger body mass than a child (requiring more maintenance energy). Although their job is sedentary, their baseline metabolic need is higher than the child's.
  • Fire-fighter: Requires the most energy because they are an adult (large mass) plus they perform very heavy physical labor (high activity), similar to the "Very active adult" in Graph C.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 5-year-old only eats buns.

a) How much would he have to eat each day to get the energy he needs?

From Graph C, a 5-year-old needs approximately 6,000 kJ (reading the bar between 4000 and 8000).

Using data from the previous page (Bun = 544 kJ):

$$ \text{Number of buns} = \frac{6000}{544} \approx 11 \text{ buns} $$

Answer: He would need to eat approximately 11 buns per day.

b) If he only ate falafel burgers, how much would he have to eat each day?

From previous page data (Falafel burger = 1990 kJ):

$$ \text{Number of burgers} = \frac{6000}{1990} \approx 3 \text{ burgers} $$

Answer: He would need to eat approximately 3 burgers per day.

c) Why shouldn't you always eat only one type of food?

  • Nutrient Deficiency: One type of food rarely contains all the essential nutrients (vitamins, minerals, protein, fibre) needed for health.
  • Health Issues: Eating only buns (carbohydrates/fats) would lead to deficiencies (e.g., Scurvy from lack of Vitamin C) and poor growth. A balanced diet requires a mixture of different foods.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Explain the link between the amount of food someone eats, the amount of activity they carry out and the amount of weight they gain.

Weight gain occurs when there is an energy imbalance.

  • If a person eats a large amount of food (High Energy In) but carries out very little activity (Low Energy Out), the excess energy is stored as fat.
  • Conversely, high activity levels burn more energy, reducing the likelihood of weight gain even if food intake is moderate.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Look at photo E. How will the amount of time koalas spend asleep affect the amount of food they need to find?

  • Koalas feed on eucalyptus leaves, which provide very low energy (as stated in the Fact box).
  • By sleeping for 20 hours a day, koalas minimize their energy expenditure (activity).
  • This allows them to survive on a low-energy diet because they are conserving energy rather than burning it through movement.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Scientists can measure the amount of energy stored in different foods. How can this knowledge help mountain climbers and explorers?

  • Calorimetry: Scientists use calorimetry (burning food to heat water) to measure energy content (kJ).
  • Application: Mountain climbers (like those in Photo D) have extremely high energy needs (~19,000 kJ/day) but can only carry limited weight.
  • Knowing the exact energy values allows them to pack energy-dense foods (foods with high kJ per gram), ensuring they have enough fuel for the climb without carrying excessive bulk.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this topic, you should be able to:

  • Recall that our bodies need energy, which we get from food
  • Explain why different people need different amounts of energy from food (Age, Activity, Growth)
  • Recall that the units for measuring energy are joules (J) or kilojoules (kJ), where 1 kJ = 1000 J
  • Explain the link between diet, activity, and weight gain/loss

docs.title: Key Terms
docs.text: Term Definition Balanced diet A diet that provides all nutrients in the correct proportions Energy balance The relationship between energy intake and energy expenditure Energy surplus When energy intake exceeds energy expenditure, leading to fat storage Energy deficit When energy expenditure exceeds energy intake, leading to weight loss Metabolism The chemical processes in the body that maintain life and require energy

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
• Understand how energy is stored and transferred
  • Identify different forms of energy storage
  • Explain the law of conservation of energy

docs.title: What is Energy Storage?
docs.text:
• Energy can be stored in various forms
  • Chemical energy: Energy stored in chemical substances
  • Food
  • Petrol
  • Cells (batteries)

docs.title: Energy Transfer
docs.text:
Definition: When energy moves from one store to another, it is transferred.

Energy can be transferred by: - Heating - Light - Sound - Electricity - Forces

docs.title: Kinetic Energy
docs.text:
• Moving objects store energy
  • This is called kinetic energy

docs.title: Law of Conservation of Energy
docs.text: Energy cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be transferred and stored in different ways.

docs.title: Fireworks (Photo A)
docs.text:
• Chemical substances inside firework rockets store energy
  • When the firework goes off, energy is transferred by:
  • Light
  • Sound
  • Heating

docs.title: Fairground Rides (Photo B)
docs.text:
• Powered by diesel generators
  • Energy stored in diesel fuel (chemical substances)
  • Energy transferred by:
  • Electricity
  • Forces
  • Results in kinetic energy (moving people and rides)

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Which of the rides shown in photo A needs the most energy and which the least energy? Explain your answers.

Answer: - Most energy: Large roller coasters or drops - they need to lift heavy cars with multiple passengers to great heights and move them at high speeds - Least energy: Smaller rides like carousels - they move slowly and don't need to overcome as much gravity or friction

docs.title: 2a) Five different things happening in photos A and B that need energy:
docs.text:
1. Fireworks exploding (light and sound)
  2. Fairground ride moving
  3. People walking around the park
  4. Diesel generator running
  5. Lights illuminating the rides

docs.title: 2b) How is energy provided for these things?
docs.text:
1. Fireworks: Chemical energy stored in explosives
  2. Rides: Diesel fuel → electricity and forces
  3. People: Chemical energy from food
  4. Generator: Chemical energy in diesel fuel
  5. Lights: Electrical energy from generators

docs.title: 3a) Five things done yesterday that needed energy:
docs.text:
1. Walking to school
  2. Eating breakfast
  3. Using a phone/computer
  4. Riding a bus/bicycle
  5. Studying/reading

docs.title: 3b) Most and least energy:
docs.text:
• Most energy: Walking/cycling - required physical movement using muscle power
  • Least energy: Reading - mainly mental activity with minimal physical movement
  • How I know: Physical activities make you tired and burn more calories than sedentary activities

docs.title: 1a) Three things that transfer energy by heating or light:
docs.text:
1. Sun (light and heating)
  2. Electric heater (heating)
  3. Light bulb (light)

docs.title: 1b) Three things that transfer energy by sound:
docs.text:
1. Speakers
  2. Musical instruments
  3. Fireworks

docs.title: 1c) Three things that use energy transferred by electricity:
docs.text:
1. Television
  2. Refrigerator
  3. Computer

docs.title: Question 2 (Page 138)
docs.text:
Diesel fuel is a store of energy in chemicals. Write down three other things that store energy in chemicals.

Answer: 1. Food - stores chemical energy for our bodies 2. Petrol - stores chemical energy for vehicles 3. Batteries - store chemical energy for electrical devices

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: ✓ Energy is stored in food, fuels, and batteries as chemical energy

docs.title: Energy in a Theme Park
docs.text: There are a lot of things happening in a theme park. Almost everything that happens involves energy in some way. For example, your body needs energy to stay alive, and to allow you to move around. Energy is needed to make all the rides in the theme park work. The energy needed is stored in food and in fuels, such as petrol.

docs.title: 1. Which of the rides shown in photo A needs the most energy and which the least energy? Explain your answers.

docs.text:
a) Write down five different things that are happening in photos A and B that need energy.

b) How is energy provided for these things to happen?

a) Write down five things you did yesterday that needed energy.

b) Which of these things do you think needed the most energy, and which needed the least energy? How do you know?

Note: Since photos A and B are not provided, answers to questions referencing them should be based on typical theme park observations (e.g., roller coasters, carousels, food stalls, lighting, sound systems, etc.).

docs.title: How Do We Explain the Properties of Solids, Liquids and Gases?
docs.text:
📸 Photo A: The icebreaker Xue Long ("ice dragon") frozen into the Arctic ice

Photo A shows a scientific expedition living on the frozen Arctic Ocean to study energy transfers. Ships are normally designed to be used on liquid water, but the ship in photo A has been specially designed to remain undamaged by the forces from solid water.

Materials can exist in three states of matter: solid, liquid or gas. The properties of a material are different in each of the three states.

  • Solids keep their shape and volume; they cannot flow and are difficult to compress (squash into a smaller volume).
  • Liquids keep their volume but not their shape; they can flow, take the shape of their containers and are difficult to compress.
  • Gases can change their shape and volume; they spread out in all directions and are easy to compress.

docs.title: The Particle Model
docs.text:
The properties of the different states can be explained using the particle theory or particle model. This states that all matter is made up of moving particles held together by forces of attraction. The particles can be atoms or molecules.

📊 Table B shows how the forces and movement are different in the three states.

State Forces Spacing Movement Solid Strong Close Vibrate in fixed positions Liquid Fairly strong Close Move around within the liquid Gas Weak Far apart Move about fast in all directions

(Note: The original textbook includes particle diagrams in this table. You can add them later using ![Solid](path/to/solid-diagram.png) if needed.)

docs.title: Questions
docs.text:
1. Name three different: a) solid materials b) liquids c) gases
  1. Write down one similarity and one difference between the properties of: a) solids and liquids b) liquids and gases
  1. How does the arrangement and spacing of particles explain: a) why it is difficult to compress solids and liquids b) why gases are easily compressed?
  1. How do the forces between particles explain: a) why solids keep their shape b) why gases expand to fill their containers?

docs.title: Particle Model: Expansion, Contraction and Density
Diffusion
docs.text:
Definition: Gases or liquids mix without anything moving them because the particles are moving around all the time.

  • Particles move randomly
  • No external force required
  • Occurs in gases and liquids
  • Driven by kinetic energy of particles

docs.title: Brownian Motion
docs.text:
Definition: Tiny bits of dust in air or water can be seen jiggling around as they are hit by the moving air or water particles.

  • Evidence of particle movement
  • Visible under microscope
  • Caused by collisions with invisible particles
  • Supports kinetic theory

docs.title: Expanding and Contracting
docs.text:
Heating: - Materials expand when heated - Particles move faster - Particles take up more space - Increased kinetic energy

Cooling: - Materials contract when cooled - Particles move slower - Particles take up less space - Decreased kinetic energy

docs.title: Density Changes
docs.text:
Formula: Density = Mass / Volume

When material contracts: - Same mass in smaller volume - Density increases

When material expands: - Same mass in larger volume - Density decreases

docs.title: 1. Thermometers (Photo E)
docs.text:
How they work: - Liquid expands when heated - Liquid rises up the tube - Expansion provides temperature reading - Reservoir contains liquid

Components: - Reservoir of liquid (mercury or alcohol) - Narrow tube - Temperature scale

docs.title: 2. Train Tracks (Photo D)
docs.text:
Problem: - Train tracks can buckle and twist in very hot weather - Caused by thermal expansion

Solution: - Gaps left between rail sections - Allows for expansion - Prevents buckling

docs.title: 3. Bridges (Photo F)
docs.text: Expansion Joints: - Buildings, bridges and structures change size all the time - Built with gaps (expansion joints) - Materials expand without bending or breaking - Prevents structural damage

docs.title: Solids
docs.text: Key Points: - Particles vibrate further about fixed positions - Particles themselves do not change size - Increased vibration = more space needed

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A substance cools down when energy is transferred away from it. Explain how this affects:

a) The movement of the particles b) The size of the object

Answer Guide: - Particles move slower - Object contracts (gets smaller)

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Look at Photo E (thermometer):

a) Explain how a thermometer works b) Suggest how you could make your own thermometer

Answer Guide: - Liquid expands when heated - Rises in narrow tube - DIY: Bottle, colored water, straw, seal

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A bridge is built without expansion gaps. Explain what could happen if the temperature became:

a) Much hotter than the day it was built b) Much colder than the day it was built

Answer Guide: - Hot: Bridge expands, could buckle or crack - Cold: Bridge contracts, could create stress fractures

docs.title: Learning Objectives ✓
docs.text:
By the end of this topic, you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe the properties of different states of matter
  • [ ] Explain the properties in terms of the particle model
  • [ ] Explain why materials expand and contract when temperature changes
  • [ ] Understand real-world applications of thermal expansion
  • [ ] Calculate and explain density changes

docs.title: Key Terms
docs.text: Term Definition Diffusion Mixing of gases/liquids due to particle movement Brownian Motion Random movement of particles suspended in fluid Expansion Increase in volume when heated Contraction Decrease in volume when cooled Density Mass per unit volume Expansion Joint Gap built into structures to allow for expansion

docs.title: Did You Know?
docs.text: FACT: Train tracks can buckle and twist in very hot weather if expansion gaps are not properly maintained!

docs.title: Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Remember: Particles themselves don't change size - only the space between them changes
  2. Visualize: Draw particle diagrams for hot vs cold materials
  3. Connect: Link theory to real examples (thermometers, bridges, train tracks)
  4. Practice: Explain concepts in your own words

Page Reference: 135

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Learn how to break tasks into Input → Process → Output for automation.

docs.title: What You’ll Learn
docs.text:
• How to think like a systems operator
  • Why planning beats coding first
  • Basic Python concepts (variables, logic)

docs.title: Lesson Content
docs.text:
Every automation follows this pattern:  Input — the raw data or task 2. Process — the logic to handle it 3. Output — the result you want

Example:  Task: Calculate total revenue from sales data.

  • Input: sales.csv
  • Process: sum the "revenue" column
  • Output: total revenue number

docs.title: Pro Tip:
docs.text: Before touching code, write down the steps in plain language. This is the key to thinking like a programmer without being a programmer.
